headlines.title: VILLA DENIES HE HAS SPLIT WITH CHIEF CARRANZA
headlines.fullText:
(COTINUED FROM PAGE "ONE.)
been no split and if Villa had seized the telegraph offices as reported, he acted entirely upon his authority as chief of the northern military zone, his purpose being, they said, to cen- For telegrams in relation to the nitu- ation at Zacatecas.
The report, nevertheless, aroused considerable apprehension. A split between the military and civilian lead- ers, it was pointed out, probably would result in conditions that would stir up demands for American intervention Rafael Zubaran and Luis Cabrera. the Carranza agents who met the American delegates at Buffalo yester- day, were to return to Washington to- day. When Secretary Bryan went to the White House he was expected to submit to President Wilson a full re- Port of the result of the meeting at Buffalo.

headlines.title: FIRST FT. WORTH WELL 'COMES BACK'
headlines.subtitle: City's Original Spouter Once More Reaches Flowing Level.
headlines.fullText:
Tucker Hill well, the original arte- sian well established as a municipal water supply in the eighties, has come back. Water has risen in it to the height at which it originally flowed without pumpage into the city mains. The abandoned well, which stands In the grounds of the Fifth ward fire station, now has water standing in it within a couple of feet of the top. Since the days when it was used for a city supply the ground about it has been filled in several, feet and the water is now far above the height from which It once flowed Into the maine.
When storage of water began at Lake Worth the Tucker Hill well water is expected to flow over the top before the month is out. An iron cap has been placed on it.
The Tucker Hill well started the Fort Worth artesian system. Early historical accounts tell of the excite- ment when the well burst open, the water flowing in torrents toward Texas & Pacific railway. It was con- tinued as a part of the city water system. Finally the Mead artesian system, with Its series of wells flowing into one main. Fire Chief Bideker attributes the rise to the water at Lake Worth. Though a large gap re- mains in the dam there is already a big lake formed and Bideker believes the water filters through it naturally Into the sands that supply the well.

headlines.title: MEDIATION PLAN OF U. S. MAY NOW UNDERGO CHANGE
headlines.fullText:
(CUTINVED FROM PAGE ONE.)
new administration would soon crush the constitutionalists because a recog- nized government could get financial aid and arms and without it the constitutionalists could not. The United States is unwilling to assent to this because it believes such a program would only mean a renewal of the war, with its attendant chance of injuries to foreigners and international complications.
The indications today were that the conference Friday would develop whether efforts to compose internation- al difficulties shall be continued, or whether in this event future meetings would be confined to an effort to dispose of the international conflict between the United States and Mexico.

headlines.title: PYTHIANS SEND $12 FOR TROUTMAN FUND
headlines.subtitle: Chancellor Commander Writes Governor Amount May Be Swelled.
headlines.fullText: Contribution to the fund being raised by Governor Colquitt to erect a monu- ment to the memory of Joanna Trout- man, designer and maker of the first Texas flag, was made at the meeting of Queen City lodge No. 21, Knights of Pythias, Tuesday night. The fund, amounting to $12, was remitted to the Governor Wednesday morning by H. A. Lawrence, chancellor commander of the lodge, who told him the fund might later be swelled. Other Pythians not present at the meeting are asked by Lawrence to send any contributions direct to Governor Colquitt.

headlines.title: MANY PHYSICIANS EXPECTED AT FEAST
headlines.subtitle: About 125 Will Attend Event Honoring Dr. Frank Boyd.
headlines.fullText:
It Is expected that about 125 physicians, Including twenty-five or thirty from other cities in the state, will be present at the banquet to be given by the Tar. rant County Medical Society at the Westbrook Hotel tonight in honor of Dr. Frank D. Boyd, the new president of the State Medical Association. There will be several addresses by laymen including Capt. B. B. Paddock, Rev. R. M. Nolan, Rev. William Caldwell, Dr. F. D. Kershner and Mr. J. M. Wortham.
Among the prominent representatives of the medical fraternity from other cities who will be in attendance are Drs. Hosmer, Taber and Carrick of Dallas, Dr. Irby of Weatherford and Dr. Parsons of San Antonio. An invitation is extended to all physicians in Tarrant county.

headlines.title: SHIPMENT OF BIRDS FOR ZOO IS HERE
headlines.subtitle: Total Number at Forest Park In- creased to 150 Kangaroo Ex- pected Tomorrow.
headlines.fullText: A shipment of 110 birds for the For- est Park Zoo arrived Wednesday morn- ing from Kansas City and was placed in the aviary. The total number at the zoo is now 150. The shipment included water fowl, about fifty cockatoos, eighteen red-head linnets, twelve Java gray sparrows, eighteen paraquettes, fourteen parrots and forty-one canaries. Of the entire shipment from Kansas City but four birds died. A pair of kangaroos also ordered for the zoo is expected to arrive Thurs- day.

headlines.title: COMMON CARRIER PLAN PROPOSED FOR LAYING PIPE LINE
headlines.fullText:
common carrier like those established in the West Virginia oil fields and like that now proposed for Oklahoma. By metering, he argues, one pipe line could be made to serve the pur- poses of all the companies in reaching the industrial plants and at the same time any customer could get oil from any one of them through the common carrier.
A franchise already is held by the Houston & Texas Central. The time limit provided in the fran- chise has expired and a question has been raised as to whether it cannot be forfeited. It follows practically the same terri- tory asked for by the Magnolia com- pany, showing that one general line would reach about all the territory.
Bideker suggests that the compa- nies might unite in building the line. Other companies that want to come in later would be charged a rental for its use. This plan also would do away with the objection raised to guaranteeing to build parts of the line that might not be used at this time. A single company might get a contract with an industrial plant and another company might lose it. If a common carrier line were built, no matter what company got a con- tract, the same pipe would be in serv- ice. For the city it would also have the advantage of reducing the number of times streets would be torn up to lay pipes.

headlines.title: LEE LEADS EBERHARDT
headlines.fullText:
(CONTINUED FROM PAGE ONE.)
ST. PAUL, Minn., June 17 .- Primary returns from various parts of the state today showed W. L. Lee running ahead of Governor Eberhardt for the Republican nomination for governor. Early today returns outside the Twin Cities showed 11,513 for Eberhardt and 14,458 for Lee. At this ratio Lee's lead would be 20,000 outside the Twin Cities.

headlines.title: MANY ARE ATTENDING SUMMER HIGH SCHOOLS
headlines.subtitle: Three Buildings in Use for Pupils Behind in Work.
headlines.fullText: More than 250 public school pupils are attending the three summer schools established for pupils who are behind less than one-half of a grade. The schools opened last Thursday for six weeks terms. The summer high school is at Central high school in charge of Prof. G. O. Clough. Grammar grade pupils living south of the Texas & Pacific reservation are at- tending summer school at the Alex- ander Hogg building and those living north of the tracks at the Peter Smith building. Prof. W. N. Green is in charge of the Alexander Hogg school and Prof. N. E. Randol is in charge of the Peter Smith building. The school board furnished the buildings and designated the teachers. The tuition for the high school term is $8 and for the grammar term $5. Classes are held from 8 a. m. until noon daily. About 100 are attending the high school, 125 are enrolled at the Alexan- der Hogg school and thirty or forty at the Peter Smith school.

headlines.title: C. OF C. WILL INVITE EDITORS TO COME HERE
headlines.fullText: R. H. Stansell, In charge of the con- vention bureau of the Chamber of Commerce, will leave tonight for Wich- ita Falls to invite the Northwest Texas Press Association to hold its next con- vention in Fort Worth. The convention will begin at Wich- ita Falls Thursday. Claude Callan of The Star-Telegram and Hugh Nugent Fitzgerald of the Record will attend the convention.

headlines.title: EVANS IN RACE
headlines.subtitle: Justice of Peace Candidate's Name Is Omitted in List.
headlines.fullText: In the list of candidates in Tuesday's Star-Telegram the name of Frank Evans, candidate for justice of the peace, precinct No. 1, was omitted. Evans drew fifth place on the ballot.

headlines.title: PILOTS OF LOST BALLOON ARE SAFE IN MOUNTAIN CAMP
headlines.fullText:
(CONTINUED FROM PAGE ONE.)
the forest rangers' camp at Walker's Prairie today.
The Springfield was forced to de- scend at 8 o'clock last Friday morning about eighteen miles from Walker's Prairie ranger station. They had been living on half rations of uncooked food. Donaldson said the balloon was wrecked.
Homer Williams and A. Mitchell, the forest guards stationed at the camp, said the two aeronauts staggered up one of the paths leading from a nearby creek and fell exhausted at the cabin door. Their clothing was in shreds. Unlike the three other crews in the race which started from Portland last Wednesday, Donaldson and Henderson were not compelled to dodge the spikes of mountain peaks, but they found plenty of hardships when they started to come out. They struck out for the nearest stream, and followed it westward. Having no matches, they could not build a fire and had to eat what food they had raw. The eighteen-mile walk to Walker's Prairie was one of hardship. Dense undergrowth and fallen trees disputed every foot of the way. No attempt was made to save the balloon.

headlines.title: COMPANY TO INSURE LIVESTOCK FORMED
headlines.subtitle: Reciprocal Plan Will Be Followed. Will Write Policies
headlines.fullText:
Lester G. Ocheltree, 609 Wheat build- ing, was granted a permit by the department of insurance at Austin Wednesday for the Texas Livestock Insurance Association to do busi- ness. Ocheltree will be mana- ger of the association. Further organi- zation has not been perfected yet. The association is reciprocal. It has no capital stock. Members will be as- sessed if premiums do not pay losses on livestock that dies, is killed or stolen.
The association will begin writing pol- icies in the fall.
There is one other such association in Texas. Its headquarters is at Dallas.

headlines.title: Out at Wayside Farm There Is a Smile for Candidates
headlines.subtitle: C. B. Law, Who Once Ran for Office, Is the Only Original and Forever Friend of the Political Aspirant in Tarrant County.
headlines.fullText:
Seven miles' spin out the Azle cardinal road of the "million dollar system" brings you to the Wayside Farm.
If you are a candidate for public office in Tarrant county, drop your reins or stop your engine, for this is your home. Never mind about hitch- ing old Dobbin or slamming your car door. Everything is safe without hitching at Wayside Farm.
There's a reason for all this strange welcome for a candidate. The boss of Wayside Farm is the friend of every candidate; he really is glad to see him and he gives him a country supper that makes him for- get his troubles for a while, unless he be like County Clerk Lion Beavers says he is, a candidate for your good will.
One time C. B. Law was a candi- date. He ran for county tax assessor against "Uncle Bob" Davis, the pres- ent Fort Worth police commissioner, when "Uncle Bob" first ran for the assessor's job. Law was defeated, but he learned "what the candidate is up against" and he says he never can be mean to a candidate again as long as he lives.
"Anyhow," he says, "I didn't mind being beat. I got the experience and I had a summer campaign and I wouldn't care how often I got licked. I enjoy the life of a candidate, although the candidate has a hard time of it."
He Always Sympathized.
Before he was a candidate, though, Law had an instinctive sympathy for candidates. Ten years ago he was elected a member of the county Dem- ocratic executive committee and it was with this modest advent in poli- tics that he put up a "bulletin board" on the road in front of his house. The humble highway itself in those days.
This year the Azle cardinal road, part of the million dollar system, has been built and Law has replaced the old board with a handsomer board. This board contains all sorts of information valuable to the candidate. It tells the candidate that the election box is named Way- side and that it is at the school house down the road.
Then there is a big space for can- didates' cards and his slogan, "Your vote and influence." Scattered around the board are several hundred superfluous cards.
Has Only Bulletin Board.
"I have the only bulletin board in Tarrant county, inside or outside of Fort Worth," is Law's proud boast. "We have the only candidate's friend in Tarrant county, outside of Fort Worth," some of the candidates say. Several days ago candidates kept dropping in at Wayside Farm until there were seven for dinner and Law fed them and told them funny stories and gave them a new inspiration for work.
In the last campaign Law invited thirty-five candidates to take break- fast with him on a certain morning. All thirty-five piled in for supper. There was no supper for them, but they cleaned out the cellar, the pantry and the garden and Law was glad to have them at that.

headlines.title: College Degree Given 'Student' After 29 Years
headlines.fullText:
By Associated Press.
CHAMPAIGN, ILL., June 17.—Sus- pended twenty-nine years ago because he would not attend military drill, William F. North, a member of the class of '85, received his degree of bachelor of science from the University of Illinois today.
President Edmund J. James of the university came on the papers in the forgotten case a short time ago when he was engaged in rounding up the old alumni for a home- coming event. He wrote to North, embittered by a vain fight that had taken his last dollar years ago and had ended only in the state court of appeals, asking him to come back and get his degree. North wrote back curtly that all he wished the university to do was to forget him.
President James wrote again that the rule was abrogated now and wanted him to come and get his degree. North came here today.

headlines.title: EFFORT TO ARRANGE DEBATE BEING MADE
headlines.subtitle: Substitute Will Represent Ferguson If McDonald Can't Be Found.
headlines.fullText:
Secretary J. E. Mercer of the Tar- rant County Ferguson Democratic Club was trying Wednesday to get in communication with C. C. McDonald of El Paso, former secretary of state, and F. F. Hill of Denton county, to arrange a joint debate.
If McDonald cannot be reached, Mercer said Wednesday that some other speaker will be offered to debate with Hill, whose challenge to McDonald was sent to Mercer by R. Hanna Ridgway, secretary of the Tarrant County Tom Ball Club.
McDonald left Fort Worth Tuesday to fill speaking dates in the West after speaking here Monday night in the in- terest of Ferguson's candidacy for gov- ernor.

headlines.title: PEOPLE FLEEING FROM FLOOD AT CHRISTOVAL
headlines.subtitle: Sudden Rise of Fifteen Feet Sweeps Down Concho River and Many Houses Are Inundated.
headlines.fullText:
SAN ANGELO, June 17.—Many of the residents of Christoval, twenty miles to the southward, have fled from their homes. A sudden rise of fifteen feet in the Concho river has threatened to sweep the town off the map.
Many of the houses in the lower por- tion of the town are under water.

headlines.title: CONTRACTORS MUST GET PERMITS FIRST
headlines.subtitle: Plan Hitherto Allowed to Save Time Will Be Stopped—Abused Is Claimed.
headlines.fullText:
Notice was issued by City Commis- sioner Allen Wednesday morning that hereafter there will be strict enforce- ment of the regulation requiring elec- trical contractors to take out permits for all work started. To prevent delays in work, contrac- tors have been permitted to start work without getting permits, notifying the work department when the work was done. It is claimed some of them failed to notify the city and the new order will require permits before starting work in all cases.
These permits are issued by the city electrician, who later inspects the work and passes upon it.

headlines.title: DAY IN CONGRESS
headlines.fullText:
WASHINGTON, June 17.—
Senate: Met at noon. Debate was resumed on Indian appropriation bill.
Senator Jones presented petitions from the Seattle Chamber of Commerce and others protesting against antitrust legislation.
House: Met at noon.
Consideration of the judiciary code revision bill was resumed.
The foreign affairs committee voted favorably to report the resolution in- viting foreign nations to participate in the International Dry Farming Con- gress at Wichita, Kan., Oct. 7-17.

headlines.title: DON CAMPBELL'S WILL FILED FOR PROBATE
headlines.subtitle: Estate Estimated to Be Worth $50,000—Son to Get $100 and Widow Rest.
headlines.fullText: The will of the late Don Campbell was filed for probate Wednesday by his widow, Mrs. Fayrene Campbell. The will which was made July 30, 1907, leaves $100 to his son, Argyle Campbell, and the rest of the estate to his wife, who is also named as exe- cutrix. The value of the estate is esti- mated at $50,000.

headlines.title: DEVELOPERS MEET
headlines.subtitle: East Texas Association Opens Session at Timpson.
headlines.fullText:
TIMPSON, Tex., June 17.—The third annual convention of the East Texas Development Association convened here today. The attendance promises to exceed that of any previous meet- ing. Tucker Royal of Palestine is pres- ident and R. S. Bolton of Jacksonville sec- retary of the association.
The program formally opened this afternoon with addresses of welcome.

headlines.title: SHERMAN BOOSTERS AFTER INTERURBAN
headlines.fullText:
Sherman boosters who will visit Fort Worth 150 strong next Tuesday to sound the sentiment in Fort Worth for the extension of the Fort Worth-Den- ton interurban to Sherman. The Sherman boosters will visit Denton first.
The boosters, traveling on a special train, will be accompanied by the Weinstein Silver Cornet Band of Sher- man and Denison.
